title: How to Work with Perfectionism That Surfaces During Journeys
body:
How to Work with Perfectionism That Surfaces During Journeys

So, you’re on a psychedelic journey, and suddenly, that voice creeps in. You know the one. The one that says, “You’re not doing this right,” or “You should be feeling something different.” Perfectionism. Yep. It can show up at the worst times, right?! Like when you’re supposed to be surrendering, opening up, or just letting go. Instead, you’re stuck wrestling with a judgmental inner coach who refuses to shut up.

Look, perfectionism during journeys is more common than you think. I’m Kalesh, and I’ve worked with hundreds of people navigating this exact challenge. The trick isn’t to fight it or pretend it’s not there. That only makes things worse. Here’s the thing: perfectionism is often a defense mechanism. It’s your brain’s way of trying to control the uncontrolled, to bring order to what feels chaotic or vulnerable. But in a journey, control is an illusion. So how do you work with this stubborn part, instead of against it? Let’s talk specifics.

Why Perfectionism Shows Up During Journeys

First, you need to understand why this happens. Psychedelic journeys, especially the deeper ones, strip away your usual mental frameworks. Your ego’s usual defenses get softened. Many people expect to “feel enlightened” or “reach a breakthrough.” When the experience doesn’t match those expectations, perfectionism kicks in. It’s the part of you that says, “I’m failing” or “I’m not good enough at this.”

It’s not just about feeling “good.” Sometimes, perfectionism is about the fear of vulnerability itself. When your mind treads unfamiliar territory, that critical voice shows up to protect you from feeling exposed or out of control. Know what I mean?

Here’s a concrete example: a client named Jamie told me after a recent trip, “I felt like I wasn’t ‘doing’ the journey right. I kept trying to force insights, but nothing came. I started beating myself up for being stuck.” That’s perfectionism in action, disguised as motivation but really just a trap.

Step One: Acknowledge the Perfectionism Without Judgment

Okay, so you notice the perfectionist voice during your journey. What now? Don’t push it away or try to silence it. That just makes it louder. Instead, acknowledge it. Say internally, “Oh, there you are. I see you.” Don’t agree or disagree. Just notice.

This is where grounding tools come in handy. Something like a weighted blanket for grounding (paid link) can provide a physical anchor. It’s like telling your nervous system, “Hey, it’s okay. You’re safe here.” Even in the middle of a trip, feeling grounded can help you observe perfectionism more clearly, rather than getting caught in its grip.

Try this: When the critical voice pops up, take three deep breaths. Feel your feet on the floor or the texture of your blanket. Let the perfectionism be there without buying into its story. This isn’t magic, but it’s effective.

Step Two: Shift From “Doing” to “Being”

Perfectionism loves action. It wants you to do something - analyze, fix, control. But journeys aren’t about doing. They’re about being. Here’s where a meditation cushion, like a meditation zafu cushion (paid link), can help. Sitting on one before and after your journey encourages you to drop into presence, not performance.

Practice this: Before you begin your journey, sit quietly on your cushion for 10 minutes. Notice whatever comes up - expectations, fears, excitement. Don’t try to change them. Just be with them. This trains your mind to rest in presence, so when the perfectionism kicks in later, you’re better equipped not to react.

Truth is, perfectionism thrives where there’s resistance. The more you try to “get it right,” the more stuck you become. Instead, try this mantra: “I don’t have to do anything. I just have to be here.” Repeat it internally whenever you feel pressure to perform.

Step Three: Reframe Your Relationship With the Critical Voice

Here’s a tool that’s surprisingly powerful. When perfectionism shows up, imagine it as a character. Give it a name, a personality. Maybe it’s “The Drill Sergeant,” or “Ms. Critique.” Give it a voice, but don’t let it run the show.

Jamie, the client I mentioned, started calling her inner critic “Karen.” Whenever Karen got loud, Jamie would say, “Thanks for your input, Karen. I hear you, but I’m gonna try a different approach now.” This kind of playful detachment changes the dynamic. Instead of battling the voice, you’re having a conversation with it.

It’s also okay to set boundaries with this voice. You can say mentally, “I appreciate your concern, but I’m not going to listen to you right now.” Try it next time you feel overwhelmed by perfectionism during a journey. Does that land?

Step Four: Embrace Imperfection as a Sign of Progress

Here’s something I want you to really sit with. Imperfection isn’t a failure. It’s proof you’re alive and growing. Every journey is unique. Sometimes, the biggest insights come from moments you thought were “off” or “wrong.”

For example, I remember a friend, Lena, who once told me, “My trip was a mess. I cried, got scared, and felt like I was spiraling. But later, I realized those were the moments that broke open my stuck patterns.”

Perfectionism often wants you to skip the messy parts. But those messy parts might be exactly where the healing happens. So next time your journey feels imperfect or confusing, lean into it. Say, “This is exactly what I need right now.”

Step Five: Integrate With Compassion and Practical Tools

After your journey, integration is where perfectionism can sneak back in. You might feel pressure to “make the most” of your experience or to “fix” yourself immediately. That’s exhausting and unrealistic.

Integration is a process, not a project. Be kind to yourself. If journaling feels overwhelming, just jot down one word that sums up your day. If meditation feels like a chore, try a walk outside instead.

One practical way to support integration is through growing your own mushrooms at home. It’s grounding, nurturing, and connects you to the rhythm of growth without pressure. Check out a mushroom growing kit (paid link). It’s a gentle reminder that growth takes time and patience. No rushing allowed.

Don’t Be Afraid to Reach Out

Perfectionism can isolate you. You might feel like you’re the only one struggling. But you’re not. Many people, including seasoned journeyers, deal with this. Sometimes, talking to someone who understands can make all the difference.

If you want some guidance on working with these inner voices, I’ve written more about working with inner critics during psychedelic journeys. It’s a resource I recommend checking out when perfectionism feels like it’s running the show.

And if you want to read more about how psychedelics impact the brain and mental health, this study from the National Institutes of Health offers solid science behind the experience.

Final Thoughts: You’re Not Alone in This

Perfectionism during journeys can feel like a trap. But it’s just a part of your mind trying to keep you safe, even if it’s misguided. The goal isn’t to silence perfectionism or beat it down. It’s to meet it with curiosity, compassion, and a little humor.

Look, if you want to learn more about working with your mind and consciousness, swing by my site. We’re all figuring this out together, one imperfect step at a time.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
